**Rochester**[[a]](#cite_note-3) is a city in and the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Monroe County, New York](/wiki/Monroe_County,_New_York "Monroe County, New York"), United States. It is the [fourth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_in_New_York "List of municipalities in New York") in New York, with a population of 211,328 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census").[[3]](#cite_note-4) The [Rochester metropolitan area](/wiki/Rochester_metropolitan_area,_New_York "Rochester metropolitan area, New York") in [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York") has an estimated 1.06 million residents and is the [54th-largest metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area") in the U.S. Throughout its history, Rochester has acquired several nicknames based on local industries; it has been known as "[the Flour City](/wiki/History_of_Rochester,_New_York#Rochesterville_and_The_Flour_City "History of Rochester, New York")" and "[the Flower City](/wiki/History_of_Rochester,_New_York#The_Flower_City "History of Rochester, New York")" for its dual role in flour production and floriculture,[[4]](#cite_note-:1-5) and as the "World's Image Center" for its association with film, optics, and photography.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

The city was one of the United States' first [boomtowns](/wiki/Boomtown "Boomtown"), initially due to the fertile [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") valley which gave rise to numerous flour mills, and then as a manufacturing center, which spurred further rapid population growth.[[6]](#cite_note-7) Rochester has also played a key part in US history as a hub for social and political movements, especially [abolitionism](/wiki/Abolitionism "Abolitionism"),[[7]](#cite_note-8) and the [women's rights movement](/wiki/Women%27s_Suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's Suffrage in the United States").[[8]](#cite_note-9)

Rochester is the birthplace and/or home of many notable companies including [Eastman Kodak](/wiki/Eastman_Kodak "Eastman Kodak"), [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox"), [Bausch & Lomb](/wiki/Bausch_%26_Lomb "Bausch & Lomb"), [Wegmans](/wiki/Wegmans "Wegmans"), [Constellation Brands](/wiki/Constellation_Brands "Constellation Brands"), [Gannett](/wiki/Gannett "Gannett"), [Paychex](/wiki/Paychex "Paychex"), [Western Union](/wiki/Western_Union "Western Union") and [Ray-Ban](/wiki/Ray-Ban "Ray-Ban"). In part due to their influence, the region became a global center for science, technology, and research and development. This has been aided by the presence of several internationally renowned universities, notably the [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester") and [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology") (RIT), and their research programs; these schools, along with many other smaller colleges, have played an increasingly large role in its economy.[[9]](#cite_note-10) The city experienced significant population decline due to [deindustrialization](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") in the late 20th century, although less severely than its [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") peers. The Rochester metropolitan area is the third-largest regional economy in New York, after [New York City](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") and [Buffalo–Niagara Falls](/wiki/Buffalo%E2%80%93Niagara_Falls_metropolitan_area "Buffalo–Niagara Falls metropolitan area").[[10]](#cite_note-11)[[11]](#cite_note-12)

Rochester is also known for its culture; in particular, the [Eastman School of Music](/wiki/Eastman_School_of_Music "Eastman School of Music"), one of the most prestigious conservatories in the world, and the [Rochester International Jazz Festival](/wiki/Rochester_International_Jazz_Festival "Rochester International Jazz Festival") anchor a vibrant music industry.[[12]](#cite_note-13) It is the site of several museums such as [The Strong National Museum of Play](/wiki/The_Strong "The Strong") and the [George Eastman Museum](/wiki/George_Eastman_Museum "George Eastman Museum"), which houses the oldest photography collection in the world.[[13]](#cite_note-14)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Rochester, New York](/wiki/History_of_Rochester,_New_York "History of Rochester, New York")

### 18th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 18th century")]

The [Seneca](/wiki/Seneca_people "Seneca people") tribe of the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy") lived around Rochester prior to the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), and used the area as a hunting ground.[[14]](#cite_note-15) Allied with the British, the Seneca were forced to cede or sell most of their land in New York after the war. The area now occupied by Rochester was ceded in the [Phelps and Gorham Purchase](/wiki/Phelps_and_Gorham_Purchase "Phelps and Gorham Purchase") of 1788. As a reward for their loyalty to the British crown, the Iroquois were given a large [land grant](/wiki/Haldimand_Proclamation "Haldimand Proclamation") on the [Grand River](/wiki/Grand_River_(Ontario) "Grand River (Ontario)") in Canada.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17)

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 19th century")]

Rochester was founded shortly after by a wave of English-Puritan-descended immigrants from [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), who were looking for new agricultural land. They were the dominant cultural group in Rochester for over a century.[[17]](#cite_note-18) On November 8, 1803, three men from [Hagerstown, Maryland](/wiki/Hagerstown,_Maryland "Hagerstown, Maryland"), purchased a 100-acre (40-[ha](/wiki/Hectare "Hectare")) tract from [the Pulteney Estate](/wiki/The_Pulteney_Association "The Pulteney Association") along the Genesee River: Major Charles Carroll, Colonel William Fitzhugh Jr, and Colonel [Nathaniel Rochester](/wiki/Nathaniel_Rochester "Nathaniel Rochester"), the namesake of the city. They chose the site because its three [cataracts](/wiki/Waterfall#Types_of_waterfalls "Waterfall") on the Genesee offered great potential for water power. Beginning in 1811, and with a population of 15, the three founders surveyed the land and laid out streets and tracts.[[18]](#cite_note-19) In 1817, the Brown brothers and other landowners joined their lands with the Hundred Acre Tract to form the village of Rochesterville. This name was unpopular, and in 1822 it was shortened to Rochester.[[19]](#cite_note-20)

By 1821, Rochesterville became the seat of Monroe County.[[20]](#cite_note-21) In 1823, the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") aqueduct over the Genesee River was completed, connecting the city to the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River") to the east.[[21]](#cite_note-22) New commerce from the canal turned the village into America's first [boomtown](/wiki/Boomtown "Boomtown").[[22]](#cite_note-23) By 1830, Rochester's population had grown to 9,200,[[23]](#cite_note-auto1-24) and in 1834, it was rechartered as a city.[[24]](#cite_note-25) Rochester was first known as "the Young Lion of the West", and then as the "Flour City". By 1838, it was the largest flour-producing city in the United States.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-5) A series of religious revivals occurred as part of the [Second Great Awakening](/wiki/Second_Great_Awakening "Second Great Awakening"), including a particularly notable revival led by [Charles Grandison Finney](/wiki/Charles_Grandison_Finney "Charles Grandison Finney") which inspired local social reform movements.[[25]](#cite_note-26)

During the mid-19th century, as the center of the wheat-processing industry moved west with population and agriculture, the city became home to an expanding [nursery](/wiki/Plant_nursery "Plant nursery") business, giving rise to the city's second nickname, the Flower City. Nurseries ringed the city, the most famous of which was started in 1840 by immigrants [George Ellwanger](/wiki/George_Ellwanger "George Ellwanger") from Germany and [Patrick Barry](/wiki/Patrick_Barry_(horticulturist) "Patrick Barry (horticulturist)") from Ireland.[[26]](#cite_note-27) [Shoemaking](/wiki/Shoemaking "Shoemaking") also became a major local industry as the city began to industrialize.[[27]](#cite_note-industry-28)

In 1847, [Frederick Douglass](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass "Frederick Douglass") founded *[The North Star](/wiki/The_North_Star_(anti-slavery_newspaper) "The North Star (anti-slavery newspaper)")*, an [abolitionist](/wiki/Abolitionism_in_the_United_States "Abolitionism in the United States") newspaper, in Rochester. A former slave and an antislavery speaker and writer, he gained a circulation of over 4,000 subscribers in the United States, Europe, and the Caribbean.[[28]](#cite_note-29) Douglass lived in Rochester until his home was destroyed in a fire in 1872, and a historical marker was erected at the site on South Avenue.[[29]](#cite_note-30) Many other prominent abolitionists operated in the area and operated on the [Underground Railroad](/wiki/Underground_Railroad "Underground Railroad"), such as [Thomas James](/wiki/Thomas_James_(minister) "Thomas James (minister)") and [Austin Steward](/wiki/Austin_Steward "Austin Steward").[[30]](#cite_note-31)

Around the same time, the nearby Finger Lakes region was the birthplace of the [women's suffrage movement](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage_in_the_United_States "Women's suffrage in the United States"). A [critical suffragettes' convention](/wiki/Seneca_Falls_Convention "Seneca Falls Convention") was held in 1848 in nearby Seneca Falls, and Rochester was the home of [Susan B. Anthony](/wiki/Susan_B._Anthony "Susan B. Anthony") along with other notable Suffragettes such as [Abigail Bush](/wiki/Abigail_Bush "Abigail Bush") and [Amy Post](/wiki/Amy_Post "Amy Post"). The city itself played host to the [Rochester Women's Rights Convention of 1848](/wiki/Rochester_Women%27s_Rights_Convention_of_1848 "Rochester Women's Rights Convention of 1848").[[31]](#cite_note-32) The [Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution](/wiki/Nineteenth_Amendment_to_the_United_States_Constitution "Nineteenth Amendment to the United States Constitution"), in 1920, which guaranteed the right of women to vote, was known as the Susan B. Anthony Amendment because of her work toward its passage, which she did not live to see.[[32]](#cite_note-33) Anthony's home is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") known as the [National Susan B. Anthony Museum and House](/wiki/Susan_B._Anthony_House "Susan B. Anthony House").[[33]](#cite_note-34)

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 20th century")]

Rochester saw an expansion of new industries in the late 19th century and early 20th century. Irish immigrant James Cunningham founded the [carriagemaker](/wiki/Carriagemaker "Carriagemaker") [James Cunningham, Son and Company](/wiki/James_Cunningham,_Son_and_Company "James Cunningham, Son and Company").[[34]](#cite_note-35) James Cunningham and Sons later founded the [Cunningham Car Company](/wiki/Cunningham_Car_Company "Cunningham Car Company"), a pioneer automobile maker.[[35]](#cite_note-36) German immigrants [John Jacob Bausch](/wiki/John_Jacob_Bausch "John Jacob Bausch") and [Henry Lomb](/wiki/Henry_Lomb "Henry Lomb") launched [Bausch & Lomb](/wiki/Bausch_%26_Lomb "Bausch & Lomb") in 1861 and inventor and entrepreneur [George Eastman](/wiki/George_Eastman "George Eastman") founded [Eastman Kodak](/wiki/Eastman_Kodak "Eastman Kodak") in 1892. In 1895, trucking executive [George F. Roth](/wiki/George_Francis_Roth "George Francis Roth") and others founded the Rochester Cash Register Company,[[36]](#cite_note-37) and obtained a patent for an "improvement in cash registers". Their company failed, however, in 1899, and its patents were sold to the [National Cash Register Company](/wiki/National_Cash_Register_Company "National Cash Register Company").[[37]](#cite_note-38)

[Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox") was founded in Rochester in 1906 as the Haloid Company.[[38]](#cite_note-39) In the early 20th century, Rochester became a center of the garment industry, particularly men's fashions. It was the base of [Bond Clothing Stores](/wiki/Bond_Clothing_Stores "Bond Clothing Stores"), [Fashion Park Clothes](/wiki/Fashion_Park_Clothes "Fashion Park Clothes"), [Hickey Freeman](/wiki/Hickey_Freeman "Hickey Freeman"), and Stein-Bloch and Co.[[27]](#cite_note-industry-28) The Erie Canal was rerouted south of Rochester by 1918 to allow widening as part of the [Barge Canal](/wiki/New_York_State_Barge_Canal "New York State Barge Canal")'s construction.[[39]](#cite_note-40) The short-lived [Rochester subway](/wiki/Rochester_subway "Rochester subway") was constructed in the abandoned canal bed and operated from 1927 to 1956.[[40]](#cite_note-subway-41)

The dawn of the 20th century in Rochester saw rapid growth, driven by waves of immigrants arriving from Germany, Italy, Poland, and elsewhere.[[41]](#cite_note-42) The city also grew in area, annexing suburban neighborhoods from the surrounding towns to arrive at its present borders.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The population reached 62,386 in 1870, 162,608 in 1900, and 295,750 in 1920. By 1950, the population had reached a high of 332,488.[[43]](#cite_note-census1-44) The surge in new arrivals, along with increased [industrialization](/wiki/Industrialization "Industrialization"), resulted in the city becoming a hotbed of labor activism.[[44]](#cite_note-45) From the 1920s and continuing into the post-war era Rochester grew into a power center for newly formed industrial unions.[[45]](#cite_note-46) It was one of the very few American cities where the labor movement was powerful enough to mount a successful [general strike](/wiki/General_strike "General strike") when in 1946 an estimated 50,000 workers across multiple sectors walked off in support of hundreds of city employees who had been fired for attempting to unionize.[[46]](#cite_note-47)[[47]](#cite_note-48)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Rochester_Downtown_-_Late_1930s.jpg/250px-Rochester_Downtown_-_Late_1930s.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochester_Downtown_-_Late_1930s.jpg)

Rochester in 1938

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), Rochester factories produced a variety of goods for the war effort, including fuel tanker ships, optical equipment, and radio proximity fuses, amounting to [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")1,200,000,000 of military orders.[[48]](#cite_note-49) Following the war, the city began engaging in [urban renewal](/wiki/Urban_renewal "Urban renewal") projects to revitalize downtown, including the construction of [Midtown Plaza](/wiki/Midtown_Plaza_(Rochester,_New_York) "Midtown Plaza (Rochester, New York)") and freeways like the [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)"), and the demolition of the Front Street neighborhood.[[49]](#cite_note-50) By the 1970s, the city experienced highway revolts against new projects,[[50]](#cite_note-51) and in the 2010s, the city began filling in the Inner Loop to restore older neighborhoods.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

In 1950, the Census Bureau reported Rochester's population as 97.6% White and 2.3% Black.[[43]](#cite_note-census1-44) Rochester's black population tripled to more than 25,000 during the 1950s. Casually employed by the city's major industries, most African Americans in the city held low-pay and low-skill jobs, and lived in substandard housing. Discontent exploded in the three-day [1964 Rochester race riot](/wiki/1964_Rochester_race_riot "1964 Rochester race riot"), which resulted in five deaths, 350 injuries, nearly a thousand arrests, and 204 stores looted or damaged.[[52]](#cite_note-Goodman_2014-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) In the wake of the riot, the Rochester Area Churches, together with black civil rights leaders, invited [Saul Alinsky](/wiki/Saul_Alinsky "Saul Alinsky") of the [Industrial Areas Foundation](/wiki/Industrial_Areas_Foundation "Industrial Areas Foundation") to help the community organize. With the Reverend [Franklin Florence](/wiki/Franklin_Florence "Franklin Florence"), they established [FIGHT](/wiki/FIGHT_(Rochester,_New_York) "FIGHT (Rochester, New York)") (Freedom, Integration, God, Honor, Today), which successfully brought pressure to bear on Eastman Kodak to help open up employment and city governance.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)

In 1992, Rochester tried to help the closing factories by turning the historic High Falls industrial area into a tourist "Entertainment District" [[56]](#cite_note-57). They built a laser light show over the waterfall, as well as a "Center at High Falls Museum" and brought in restaurants and nightclubs. While it was a hit in the 1990s, the district struggled to stay profitable by the early-2000s as businesses continued to move out.

In 1997, Kodak announced it would be laying off 10,000 employees globally, with a huge portion of those in Rochester [[57]](#cite_note-58). This was a largest job cut announcement in U.S. history at the time. It signaled to the city that the era of Kodak's dominance in the area was officially over.

With industrial restructuring in the later 20th century, Rochester's manufacturing workforce shrank.[[58]](#cite_note-59) Kodak, long the city's largest employer, conducted massive layoffs prior to a 2012 bankruptcy. Demographic changes also occurred, including thousands of Puerto Ricans moving to the city after World War II.[[59]](#cite_note-60)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 21st century")]

By 2022, the city's population had declined to 209,352 (although the metropolitan area was considerably larger) with 45.1% recorded as White and 38.4% as Black or African American.[[60]](#cite_note-census2018-61) Although the total population declined, new arrivals continued to move to the city and change its demographic profile. The city became a major destination for refugees in the 21st century.[[61]](#cite_note-62) In 2017, Rochester affirmed its status as a [sanctuary city](/wiki/Sanctuary_city "Sanctuary city").

In April of 2025, the Trump administration sued the city for its sanctuary city rules[[62]](#cite_note-63). A judge dismissed the case in November of the same year, but the government immediately refiled it. As of early 2026, Rochester asked the court to throw out the case again, arguing that the city has a Tenth Amendment right to choose which federal laws it helps enforce [[63]](#cite_note-64). Mayor Evans has led this charge against Trump.

Also in early 2026, New York State committed $75 million to give Rochester its first-ever state park [[64]](#cite_note-65). The state park will cover over 40 acres around the 80-foot High Falls waterfall and is part of a plan to bring more tourists and businesses back to the downtown area.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/GeneseeMist.jpg/250px-GeneseeMist.jpg)](/wiki/File:GeneseeMist.jpg)

The [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") in 2013

Rochester is located in [Upstate New York](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York"),[[65]](#cite_note-GR1-66) on the southern shore of [Lake Ontario](/wiki/Lake_Ontario "Lake Ontario"). The [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") bisects the city. According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 37.1 square miles (96 km2), of which 35.8 square miles (93 km2) are land and 1.3 square miles (3.4 km2) are covered by water (3.42%).[[1]](#cite_note-TigerWebMapServer-1) Rochester borders the towns of [Irondequoit](/wiki/Irondequoit,_New_York "Irondequoit, New York") to the north and northeast, [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton,_Monroe_County,_New_York "Brighton, Monroe County, New York") to the southeast and south, [Chili](/wiki/Chili,_New_York "Chili, New York") to the southwest, [Gates](/wiki/Gates,_New_York "Gates, New York") to the west, and [Greece](/wiki/Greece,_New_York "Greece, New York") to the northwest.

Rochester's landscape was formed by the [ice sheets](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet") during the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene") epoch. The retreating ice sheets reached a standstill at what is now the southern border of the city, melting at the same rate as they were advancing, depositing sediment along the southern edge of the ice mass. This created a line of hills, including (from west to east) Mt. Hope, the hills of Highland Park, Pinnacle Hill, and Cobb's Hill. Because the sediment of these hills was deposited into a proglacial lake, they are stratified and classified as a "[kame delta](/wiki/Kame "Kame")". A brief retreat and readvance of the ice sheet onto the delta deposited unstratified material there, creating a rare hybrid structure called "[kame moraine](/wiki/Moraine "Moraine")".[[66]](#cite_note-67)[[67]](#cite_note-:4-68) The ice sheets also created Lake Ontario, the [Genesee River](/wiki/Glacial_geology_of_the_Genesee_River "Glacial geology of the Genesee River") with its waterfalls and [gorges](/wiki/Canyon "Canyon"), [Irondequoit Bay](/wiki/Irondequoit_Bay "Irondequoit Bay"), [Sodus Bay](/wiki/Sodus_Bay "Sodus Bay"), [Braddock Bay](/wiki/Braddock_Bay "Braddock Bay"), [Mendon Ponds](/wiki/Mendon_Ponds_Park "Mendon Ponds Park"), numerous local streams and ponds, [the Ridge](/wiki/Ridge_Road_(Western_New_York) "Ridge Road (Western New York)"), and the nearby [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes").[[67]](#cite_note-:4-68)

Water to the city is sourced from [Hemlock Lake](/wiki/Hemlock_Lake "Hemlock Lake"), [Canadice Lake](/wiki/Canadice_Lake "Canadice Lake"), and Lake Ontario.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

### Neighborhoods

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Neighborhoods")]

Celebrate City Living, a partnership program between the city government and Rochester Coalition for Neighborhood Living, defines 35 neighborhoods in Rochester. Among these are the 14621 Community, 19th Ward, Beechwood, Browncroft, [Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_Rochester,_New_York "Charlotte, Rochester, New York"), Cobbs Hill, [Corn Hill](/wiki/Corn_Hill,_Rochester,_New_York "Corn Hill, Rochester, New York"), [downtown Rochester](/wiki/Downtown_Rochester "Downtown Rochester"), Dutchtown, [Edgerton](/wiki/Edgerton_(neighborhood) "Edgerton (neighborhood)"), EMMA (East Main, Mustard & Atlantic Avenue), High Falls, Highland Park, Homestead Heights, JOSANA (Jay-Orchard Street Area), Lincoln Park, Lyell-Otis, Maplewood, Marketview Heights, Mayor's Heights, Monroe Village, NOTA (Neighborhood of the Arts), North Winton Village, Northland-Lyceum, Park Avenue, Plymouth-Exchange, South Wedge, Susan B. Anthony, Swillburg, Upper Falls, Upper Monroe, and Upper Mount Hope.[[69]](#cite_note-70)[[70]](#cite_note-71)

#### 19th Ward

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 19th Ward")]

The 19th Ward is a southwest neighborhood bordered by Genesee Street, West Avenue, and the Erie Canal, and is across the river from the University of Rochester. The neighborhood is one of the largest in Rochester.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-73) Now known by its slogan "Urban by Choice",[[73]](#cite_note-74) in the early 19th century, the area was known as Castle Town, after Castle Inn, a tavern run by [Colonel](/wiki/Colonel "Colonel") Isaac Castle. By the early 1820s, however, the area was overshadowed by developments in the north that became downtown Rochester. Due to a tumultuous bend in the Genesee, the area was home to skilled boatsmen who assisted boats traveling north to Rochester and the area was consequently known during this time as "The Rapids".[[74]](#cite_note-75) In the 1890s, as Rochester expanded, the area rapidly urbanized. By 1930, it was a booming residential area for doctors, lawyers, and other skilled workers. Homes in the originally upper-class neighborhood typically have gumwood trim, leaded glass, fireplaces, hardwood floors, and open porches. In the 1960s, property values fell as the population of Rochester did, the area experienced [white flight](/wiki/White_flight "White flight") accelerated by school busing, [blockbusting](/wiki/Blockbusting "Blockbusting"), and [race riots](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_riots "List of ethnic riots") downtown, and crime increased, with violence, [drug use](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse"), and neglected property further diminishing property values.[[75]](#cite_note-76)[*[page needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] In recent years, neighborhood revitalization has come from the "Brooks Landing" development along the Genesee River.[[76]](#cite_note-77) [Gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") has occurred in the 19th Ward and adjacent Plymouth-Exchange area from the conversion of housing stock to student housing for the University of Rochester.[[77]](#cite_note-78) Located in the 19th Ward are the [Arvine Heights Historic District](/wiki/Arvine_Heights_Historic_District "Arvine Heights Historic District"), [Chili–West Historic District](/wiki/Chili%E2%80%93West_Historic_District "Chili–West Historic District"), [Inglewood and Thurston Historic District](/wiki/Inglewood_and_Thurston_Historic_District "Inglewood and Thurston Historic District"), and [Sibley–Elmdorf Historic District](/wiki/Sibley%E2%80%93Elmdorf_Historic_District "Sibley–Elmdorf Historic District"), listed on the National Register of Historic Places.[[78]](#cite_note-nps-79)[[79]](#cite_note-nps1-80)[[80]](#cite_note-nps2-81)

#### Browncroft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Browncroft")]

The Browncroft neighborhood is built on the former nursery grounds of the Brown Brothers nursery, between the town of Brighton and Winton Road.[[81]](#cite_note-82) Many Tudor and Colonial houses are contained within, and the business district situated on Winton Road has a mix of restaurants and shops.[[82]](#cite_note-83) The [Browncroft Historic District](/wiki/Browncroft_Historic_District "Browncroft Historic District") was listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 2004.[[83]](#cite_note-nris-84)

#### Charlotte

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Charlotte")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/RocNightAqueduct.jpg/250px-RocNightAqueduct.jpg)](/wiki/File:RocNightAqueduct.jpg)

[Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") and the historic Aqueduct [Downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Rochester "Downtown Rochester")

[Charlotte](/wiki/Charlotte,_Rochester,_New_York "Charlotte, Rochester, New York") is a lakefront community in Rochester bordering Lake Ontario. It is home to Ontario Beach Park,[[84]](#cite_note-85) commonly known as Charlotte Beach, which is a popular summer destination for Rochesterians. A new terminal was built in 2004 for the [Rochester-to-Toronto ferry service](/wiki/HSC_Dolphin_Jet "HSC Dolphin Jet") and was later sold after the ferry ceased operations in 2005. The Port of Rochester terminal still exists, but multiple attempts by the city to make additions have failed since 2016.[[85]](#cite_note-86)[[86]](#cite_note-87)

#### Corn Hill

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Corn Hill")]

[Corn Hill](/wiki/Corn_Hill,_Rochester,_New_York "Corn Hill, Rochester, New York") is located in the city's old Third Ward, and best known today for the annual [Corn Hill Arts Festival](/wiki/Corn_Hill_Arts_Festival "Corn Hill Arts Festival"). Many of the city's wealthiest residents lived in the neighborhood during the 19th century, but they relocated to the East End and suburbs after the turn of the century.[[87]](#cite_note-cornhill-88) The neighborhood experienced decline, and much of the Third Ward was demolished for the construction of I-490 in the 1960s.[[88]](#cite_note-89) Revival began in the late 1960s, as several of the Victorian homes in the neighborhood were restored by the Genesee Landmarks Foundation. In 1969 the Corn Hill arts festival was held and became an annual feature.[[87]](#cite_note-cornhill-88) The [Third Ward Historic District](/wiki/Third_Ward_Historic_District_(Rochester,_New_York) "Third Ward Historic District (Rochester, New York)") was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1974.[[83]](#cite_note-nris-84)

#### Edgerton

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Edgerton")]

The [Edgerton](/wiki/Edgerton_(neighborhood) "Edgerton (neighborhood)") neighborhood is in the city's northwest quadrant, along Lake Avenue.[[89]](#cite_note-90) Historically an Italian-American neighborhood, the area around Lyell Avenue has been a target for the formation of a designated Little Italy neighborhood, although few Italian Americans live there today.[[90]](#cite_note-91)[[91]](#cite_note-92)[[92]](#cite_note-:5-93) The neighborhood is known for struggling with a high crime rate. Residents have pursued a number of strategies to improve the neighborhood in recent years, including the construction of housing for the homeless.[[92]](#cite_note-:5-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) The neighborhood features Edgerton Park, which was once home to most of the city's professional sports teams.[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96)

#### Maplewood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Maplewood")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/RidgeRdRochesterNY.jpg/250px-RidgeRdRochesterNY.jpg)](/wiki/File:RidgeRdRochesterNY.jpg)

A commercial part of Ridge Road in Maplewood

Maplewood is located in the northwest quadrant, centered around Lake Avenue and Maplewood Park. [Eastman Business Park](/wiki/Eastman_Business_Park "Eastman Business Park") is located on its north edge. The neighborhood once contained both mansions for the wealthy and worker housing for nearby factories like Kodak's.[[96]](#cite_note-97) Many of the neighborhood's old mansions have been converted to multi-family housing.[[97]](#cite_note-98) Due to its diverse architectural heritage, the [Maplewood Historic District](/wiki/Maplewood_Historic_District "Maplewood Historic District") was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1997.[[83]](#cite_note-nris-84)

#### Marketview Heights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Marketview Heights")]

The Marketview Heights neighborhood is northeast of downtown Rochester, running east from Union Street just north of Atlantic Ave.[[98]](#cite_note-99) It is best known as the location of the Public Market, which first opened in 1905 and offers a variety of groceries and other goods from farms and shops from surrounding areas, primarily on the weekends.[[99]](#cite_note-100) In the late twentieth century, the neighborhood experienced a severe decline, and roughly half of residents lived below the poverty line in 2000. Public investment was made in the neighborhood after 2008 to implement a number of community proposals.[[100]](#cite_note-101) A second round of proposals are being explored currently for the planned removal of the Inner Loop in the southern end of the neighborhood.[[101]](#cite_note-102)

#### Park Avenue

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Park Avenue")]

Park Avenue is centered on the eponymous street southeast of downtown. It originally functioned as a service street with businesses that catered to wealthy residents who lived on nearby East Avenue.[[102]](#cite_note-parkavenue-103) Between 1894 and 1975, it was also home to Park Avenue Hospital.[[103]](#cite_note-104) Today, the neighborhood is one of the most desirable in the city, highly valued for its walkability and density of bars, clubs, and restaurants.[[102]](#cite_note-parkavenue-103) The city's annual pride parade is hosted in the neighborhood.[[104]](#cite_note-105) The Park Avenue Historic District was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2020.[[105]](#cite_note-106)

#### South Wedge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: South Wedge")]

The South Wedge is a wedge-shaped neighborhood centered on South Avenue between the Genesee River and Interstate 490. It began as the home of several families involved in trades on the Erie Canal. In the 1840s, the Ellwanger and Barry nursery was built on South Avenue, introducing greenery to the neighborhood and drawing tourists. Frederick Douglass lived in a house in the neighborhood. The area fell on hard times after World War II, when residents moved to the suburbs and several homes and businesses were abandoned. The South Wedge Planning Committee was established in 1973 to revitalize the neighborhood.[[106]](#cite_note-107) Today, the neighborhood is a hub of small businesses.[[107]](#cite_note-108) The [South Wedge Historic District](/wiki/South_Wedge_Historic_District "South Wedge Historic District") was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2013, and the Gregory Tract Historic District was listed in 2022.[[83]](#cite_note-nris-84)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Climate")]

Rochester lies in the [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") zone ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Dfa*)[[108]](#cite_note-109) and has four distinct seasons.

Winters are cold (temperatures drop to 0 °F (−18 °C) on 4.2 nights annually). Like much of the eastern Great Lakes, Rochester is very cloudy and overcast in winter. Rochester normally receives heavy snow in winter (primarily [lake effect snow](/wiki/Lake_effect_snow "Lake effect snow") resulting from its location on the southern shores of Lake Ontario), ranking among the snowiest large cities on earth[[109]](#cite_note-110) and occasionally setting records for annual snowfall among large US metros.[[110]](#cite_note-111) The 30-year annual average snowfall is just above 100 in (2.5 m).[[111]](#cite_note-NOAA-112) Spring sees plentiful rain with the rising temperatures, and occasional late snowstorms depending on the year. Summers are warm and sunny; there are occasional short periods of high heat and humidity but in general, Rochester is set apart from most of the continental US by comparatively cool, comfortable summers (ranking among the top five coolest summers among large metros alongside [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"), [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), [Portland, Oregon](/wiki/Portland,_Oregon "Portland, Oregon"), and neighboring [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York")[[112]](#cite_note-113)). Autumn features brilliant foliage colors, cooling temperatures and occasionally an excess of rain depending on the year, though precipitation is generally plentiful and dispersed fairly evenly throughout the year.

As of the [2020 United States census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), the population of Rochester was 211,328. Like most [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt") cities, the city has experienced a sustained population decline over the last 60 years. In 2020, for the first time in 200 years, Rochester dropped to the [fourth most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_New_York "List of cities in New York") in the state behind [Yonkers](/wiki/Yonkers,_New_York "Yonkers, New York"). However, in 2020, an increase in the city's population was reported for the first time since the [1950 Census](/wiki/1950_United_States_census "1950 United States census").[[117]](#cite_note-120)

As of the 2020 Census, 38.0% of Rochester residents were non-Hispanic Black, 33.0% were non-Hispanic White, 19.8% were Hispanic/Latino, 3.9% were Asian, 0.2% were Native American or Pacific Islander, and 5.1% were mixed or other.[[122]](#cite_note-Census2020-126)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Race_and_ethnicity_2020_Rochester%2C_NY.png/250px-Race_and_ethnicity_2020_Rochester%2C_NY.png)](/wiki/File:Race_and_ethnicity_2020_Rochester,_NY.png)

Map of racial distribution in Rochester, NY 2020 U.S. Census. Each dot is one person. ⬤ White ⬤ Black ⬤ Asian ⬤ Hispanic ⬤ Multiracial ⬤ Native American/other

In 2020, there were 91,500 households, of which 18.8% were married couples living together, 9.9% were unmarried co-habitating couples, 42.3% had a female householder with no partner present, and 29.0% had a male householder with no partner present. Of all households, 41.3% were made up of individuals, 25.3% had children under 18 living with them, and 9.2% had someone living alone 65 or older. 33.1% of housing units were owner-occupied, and 66.9% were rented. The age distribution was 18.8% under 18, 15.4% from 18 to 24, 30.8% from 25 to 44, 22.6% from 45 to 64, and 12.4% who were 65 or older. The [median age](/wiki/Population_pyramid "Population pyramid") was 32.9. For every 100 females, there were 93.6 males.[[122]](#cite_note-Census2020-126)

According to 2020 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey") estimates, The median income for a city household was $37,395, and for a family was $43,873. Males had a median income of $30,379, versus $28,260 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $24,916. About 25.5% of families and 30.4% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_threshold "Poverty threshold"), including 48.2% of those under age 18 and 17.3% of those age 65 or over.[[122]](#cite_note-Census2020-126)

Although losing population since 1950, over the course of the past 70 years Rochester has become a major center for immigration, particularly for arrivals from [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [Southeastern Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe"), [sub-Saharan Africa](/wiki/Sub-Saharan_Africa "Sub-Saharan Africa"), and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean"). Rochester had the highest percentage of [Puerto Ricans](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans "Puerto Ricans") of any major city in the United States in 2013,[[123]](#cite_note-factfinder2.census.gov-127) one of the four largest [Turkish American](/wiki/Turkish_American "Turkish American") communities,[[124]](#cite_note-128) one of the largest [Jamaican American](/wiki/Jamaican_American "Jamaican American") communities in any major U.S. city[[125]](#cite_note-129) and a large concentration of [Polish Americans](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") along with nearby Buffalo, New York.[[126]](#cite_note-130) Rochester's [Bhutanese](/wiki/Bhutanese_people "Bhutanese people") and [Nepalese](/wiki/Nepalis "Nepalis") communities are among the largest in the United States, concentrated primarily in Jones Square and Edgerton with growth fueled by recently arrived migrants and refugees.[[127]](#cite_note-131) In addition, Rochester was ranked number 9 in the nation for the largest Italian population in the United States in 2018.[[128]](#cite_note-132)

Rochester has been reported to have the largest *per capita* [deaf](/wiki/Hearing_loss "Hearing loss") population in the United States by the [New York Times](/wiki/New_York_Times "New York Times") because it is home to the [National Technical Institute for the Deaf](/wiki/National_Technical_Institute_for_the_Deaf "National Technical Institute for the Deaf").[[129]](#cite_note-133)[[130]](#cite_note-134) A 2012 report by the NTID concluded 3.7% of the Rochester metropolitan area is deaf or hard-of-hearing, compared to a national rate of 3.5%.[[131]](#cite_note-135) Rochester has the largest deaf and hard-of-hearing population *per capita* when analyzing the working-age population, but may not have the largest *per capita* deaf population among all cities, due to deafness being much more prevalent in the elderly.

Like many Rust Belt cities, Rochester was traditionally a manufacturing center, home to companies such as [Bausch & Lomb](/wiki/Bausch_%26_Lomb "Bausch & Lomb"), [Kodak](/wiki/Kodak "Kodak"), and [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox"). In the 21st century, [deindustrialization](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") has occurred. Xerox and Kodak each laid off thousands of workers in the 1990s and 2000s, causing the [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester") to become the city's top employer in 2005, a title it holds today.[[133]](#cite_note-138)[[134]](#cite_note-139) [Bausch & Lomb](/wiki/Bausch_%26_Lomb "Bausch & Lomb") moved to Bridgewater, New Jersey, in 2014.[[135]](#cite_note-140) The [Gannett](/wiki/Gannett_Company "Gannett Company") newspaper company and [Western Union](/wiki/Western_Union "Western Union") were founded in Rochester by [Frank Gannett](/wiki/Frank_Gannett "Frank Gannett") and [Hiram Sibley](/wiki/Hiram_Sibley "Hiram Sibley"), respectively, but have since moved to other cities. Today, the city's top employers are its educational and medical institutions. After the University of Rochester, major employers in these fields include [Rochester Regional Health](/wiki/Rochester_Regional_Health "Rochester Regional Health"), the [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology"), and [Carestream Health](/wiki/Carestream_Health "Carestream Health"). In recent years, a [high technology](/wiki/High_technology "High technology") industry has grown in Rochester, fostered in part by collaborations between private [startup enterprises](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company") and the local higher learning institutions.[[136]](#cite_note-141)[[137]](#cite_note-142) Other organizations such as High Tech Rochester provide local startups with mentorship, office space, and other resources.[[138]](#cite_note-143) Like its legacy manufacturers, Rochester's modern technological focus is on imaging and [optical science](/wiki/Optical_science "Optical science") among the industry and universities.[[139]](#cite_note-144) [The Institute of Optics](/wiki/The_Institute_of_Optics "The Institute of Optics") of the University of Rochester and the Rochester Institute of Technology have popular imaging programs,[[140]](#cite_note-145)[[141]](#cite_note-146)[[142]](#cite_note-147) and a research hub for [photonics](/wiki/Photonics "Photonics") has operated in the city since 2015 following federal and state investment.[[143]](#cite_note-148) Rochester is also home to the [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") beverage company [Constellation Brands](/wiki/Constellation_Brands "Constellation Brands")[[144]](#cite_note-149) and the [*Fortune* 1000](/wiki/Fortune_1000 "Fortune 1000") company [Paychex](/wiki/Paychex "Paychex") (Fortune #681)[[145]](#cite_note-150) and the supermarket chain [Wegmans](/wiki/Wegmans "Wegmans"). The median single-family house price was $247,000 in the third quarter of 2023 in greater Rochester, an increase of 10.3% from a year earlier, according to the [National Association of Realtors](/wiki/National_Association_of_Realtors "National Association of Realtors").[[146]](#cite_note-151)

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Rochester_-_Geva_Theatre_-_Rear_Angle.jpg/250px-Rochester_-_Geva_Theatre_-_Rear_Angle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochester_-_Geva_Theatre_-_Rear_Angle.jpg)

[Geva Theatre Center](/wiki/Geva_Theatre_Center "Geva Theatre Center") in downtown Rochester

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/LittleTheatreRochesterNewYork.JPG/250px-LittleTheatreRochesterNewYork.JPG)](/wiki/File:LittleTheatreRochesterNewYork.JPG)

The [Little Theatre](/wiki/Little_Theatre_(Rochester,_New_York) "Little Theatre (Rochester, New York)") in the East End

The city of Rochester is home to numerous cultural institutions. These include the [Garth Fagan](/wiki/Garth_Fagan "Garth Fagan") Dance, the [Rochester Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Rochester_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Rochester Philharmonic Orchestra"), the Rochester City Ballet, [Rochester Contemporary Art Center](/wiki/Rochester_Contemporary_Art_Center "Rochester Contemporary Art Center"), the Rochester Broadway Theater League, [Hochstein School of Music & Dance](/wiki/Hochstein_School_of_Music_%26_Dance "Hochstein School of Music & Dance"), the Auditorium Theater, and numerous arts organizations. [Geva Theatre Center](/wiki/Geva_Theatre_Center "Geva Theatre Center") is the city's largest professional theater. The East End Theater is on East Main Street in the theater district. The [Eastman School of Music](/wiki/Eastman_School_of_Music "Eastman School of Music"), one of the top musical institutes in the nation, and its auditorium are also within the East End neighborhood. The [Eastman Theatre](/wiki/Eastman_Theatre "Eastman Theatre") is host to the [Rochester Philharmonic Orchestra](/wiki/Rochester_Philharmonic_Orchestra "Rochester Philharmonic Orchestra") and other musical/drama events.The Rochester Association of Performing Arts is a non-profit organization that provides educational theater classes to the community.

### Architecture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Architecture")]

Several churches are among Rochester's architectural features, including [Asbury First United Methodist Church](/wiki/Asbury_First_United_Methodist_Church "Asbury First United Methodist Church"), [St. Joseph's Church and Rectory](/wiki/St._Joseph%27s_Church_and_Rectory_(Rochester,_New_York) "St. Joseph's Church and Rectory (Rochester, New York)"), and the [First Unitarian Church of Rochester](/wiki/First_Unitarian_Church_of_Rochester_(building) "First Unitarian Church of Rochester (building)"), which was designed by [Louis Kahn](/wiki/Louis_Kahn "Louis Kahn") and described by [Paul Goldberger](/wiki/Paul_Goldberger "Paul Goldberger") as one of "the most significant works of religious architecture of the century".[[147]](#cite_note-152) Significant [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") buildings include the [Cinema Theater](/wiki/Cinema_Theater_(Rochester,_New_York) "Cinema Theater (Rochester, New York)") and [Times Square Building](/wiki/Times_Square_Building_(Rochester) "Times Square Building (Rochester)"), noted for its 42' tall "Wings of Progress" sculpture. The [Midtown Plaza](/wiki/Midtown_Plaza_(Rochester) "Midtown Plaza (Rochester)"), the nation's first downtown shopping mall, first opened in 1962, and remains partially standing today.

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Museums")]

Museums in the Rochester area include the [Genesee Country Village and Museum](/wiki/Genesee_Country_Village_and_Museum "Genesee Country Village and Museum"), [George Eastman Museum](/wiki/George_Eastman_Museum "George Eastman Museum"), [Memorial Art Gallery](/wiki/Memorial_Art_Gallery "Memorial Art Gallery"), [Rochester Museum and Science Center](/wiki/Rochester_Museum_and_Science_Center "Rochester Museum and Science Center"), [Strasenburgh Planetarium](/wiki/Strasenburgh_Planetarium "Strasenburgh Planetarium"), [Susan B. Anthony House](/wiki/Susan_B._Anthony_House "Susan B. Anthony House"), [New York Museum of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_Museum_of_Transportation "New York Museum of Transportation"), [Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum](/wiki/Rochester_%26_Genesee_Valley_Railroad_Museum "Rochester & Genesee Valley Railroad Museum"), [The Strong National Museum of Play](/wiki/The_Strong_National_Museum_of_Play "The Strong National Museum of Play"), and [ARTISANworks](/wiki/ARTISANworks "ARTISANworks"). In 2023, plans were announced for a museum dedicated to the life of Frederick Douglass in the city.[[148]](#cite_note-153)

### Festivals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Festivals")]

Rochester hosts a number of cultural festivals every year. The [Lilac Festival](/wiki/Lilac_Festival_(Rochester) "Lilac Festival (Rochester)") at [Highland Park](/wiki/Highland_Park,_Rochester,_New_York "Highland Park, Rochester, New York") is attended by hundreds of thousands annually. Established after an 1898 gathering, it features the largest collection of lilac varieties in North America, a parade, and dozens of musical acts and food vendors.[[149]](#cite_note-154)[[150]](#cite_note-155) The [Rochester International Jazz Festival](/wiki/Rochester_International_Jazz_Festival "Rochester International Jazz Festival") was established in 2002 and is one of the largest [jazz festivals](/wiki/List_of_jazz_festivals "List of jazz festivals") in the United States. It takes place in late June at dozens of clubs, concert halls and free outdoor stages throughout [Downtown Rochester](/wiki/Downtown_Rochester "Downtown Rochester"), regularly drawing over 200,000 visitors.[[151]](#cite_note-156) Other notable annual festivals in Rochester include the [Rochester International Film Festival](/wiki/Rochester_International_Film_Festival "Rochester International Film Festival") in June,[[152]](#cite_note-157) the Corn Hill Arts Festival in July,[[87]](#cite_note-cornhill-88) and the [Rochester Fringe Festival](/wiki/Rochester_Fringe_Festival "Rochester Fringe Festival") in September.[[153]](#cite_note-158)

### Cuisine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Cuisine")]

See also: [History of brewing in Rochester, New York](/wiki/History_of_brewing_in_Rochester,_New_York "History of brewing in Rochester, New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Nick_Tahou%27s_Garbage_Plate.JPG/250px-Nick_Tahou%27s_Garbage_Plate.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nick_Tahou%27s_Garbage_Plate.JPG)

A white hot [Garbage Plate](/wiki/Garbage_Plate "Garbage Plate") from [Nick Tahou Hots](/wiki/Nick_Tahou_Hots "Nick Tahou Hots")

One food product Rochester calls its own is the "[white hot](/wiki/White_hot "White hot")", a variant of the [hot dog](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog") or smoked [bratwurst](/wiki/Bratwurst "Bratwurst") made by the company [Zweigle's](/wiki/Zweigle%27s "Zweigle's") and other local food manufacturers.[[154]](#cite_note-159)[[155]](#cite_note-160) Another local specialty is the [Garbage Plate](/wiki/Garbage_Plate "Garbage Plate"), a trademark of [Nick Tahou Hots](/wiki/Nick_Tahou_Hots "Nick Tahou Hots") that traditionally includes macaroni salad, home fries, and two hot dogs or cheeseburgers topped with mustard, onions, and their famous meat hot sauce. Many area restaurants feature copies or variations with the word "plate" commonly used as a general term.[[156]](#cite_note-161) [Chicken Francese](/wiki/Chicken_Francese "Chicken Francese") was first popularized by Rochester's Italian American community.[[157]](#cite_note-162)

The [Genesee Brewing Company](/wiki/Genesee_Brewing_Company "Genesee Brewing Company") is headquartered in Rochester, where it holds an annual December tradition of assembling a tower of beer kegs in the shape of a Christmas tree.[[158]](#cite_note-163) Other local franchises include [Abbott's Frozen Custard](/wiki/Abbott%27s_Frozen_Custard "Abbott's Frozen Custard"), [Bill Gray's](/wiki/Bill_Gray%27s "Bill Gray's"), [DiBella's](/wiki/DiBella%27s "DiBella's"), and [Tom Wahl's](/wiki/Tom_Wahl%27s "Tom Wahl's"). [Dinosaur Bar-B-Que](/wiki/Dinosaur_Bar-B-Que "Dinosaur Bar-B-Que"), which originated in Syracuse, also operates its second franchise downtown in the former [Lehigh Valley Railroad station](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_Railroad_Station_(Rochester,_New_York) "Lehigh Valley Railroad Station (Rochester, New York)") on the Genesee River. The [Ragú](/wiki/Rag%C3%BA "Ragú") brand of pasta sauce used to be produced in Rochester. Some of the original facility still exists and produces products for other labels (including Newman's Own) as Private Label Foods.[[159]](#cite_note-164) Rochester was also the original home of [French's](/wiki/French%27s "French's") Mustard, whose address was 1 Mustard Street.[[160]](#cite_note-165)

## Parks and recreation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Parks and recreation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Stairshighlandparkrochester2001.JPG/250px-Stairshighlandparkrochester2001.JPG)](/wiki/File:Stairshighlandparkrochester2001.JPG)

Highland Park

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/RushRheesLibraryFromMountHopeCemeteryB.jpg/250px-RushRheesLibraryFromMountHopeCemeteryB.jpg)](/wiki/File:RushRheesLibraryFromMountHopeCemeteryB.jpg)

Mount Hope Cemetery

Notable cemeteries include the Victorian-era [Mount Hope Cemetery](/wiki/Mount_Hope_Cemetery,_Rochester "Mount Hope Cemetery, Rochester"), [Holy Sepulchre](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre_Cemetery_(Rochester,_New_York) "Holy Sepulchre Cemetery (Rochester, New York)") and [Riverside Cemetery](/wiki/Riverside_Cemetery_(Rochester,_New_York) "Riverside Cemetery (Rochester, New York)"). Mount Hope became a popular picnicking destination during the late nineteenth century, and the city began to develop public parks.[[163]](#cite_note-LegacyOlmstead-168) Rochester's park system was initially designed by landscape architect [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted") in the 1890s. Olmsted intended to preserve natural landscapes for the city residents to enjoy. The system included four major parks: [Genesee Valley Park](/wiki/Genesee_Valley_Park "Genesee Valley Park"), [Highland Park](/wiki/Highland_Park_(Rochester,_New_York) "Highland Park (Rochester, New York)"), Maplewood Park, and Seneca Park,[[163]](#cite_note-LegacyOlmstead-168) the last of which is today home to the [Seneca Park Zoo](/wiki/Seneca_Park_Zoo "Seneca Park Zoo").

The park system was expanded in the early twentieth century with Cobb's Hill Park and [Durand Eastman Park](/wiki/Durand_Eastman_Park "Durand Eastman Park") in 1908, Edgerton Park in 1911, and Ontario Beach Park in the early 1920s. The Park Commission also constructed new facilities in all parks, filling them with playgrounds, pavilions, and sports facilities. Modern additions to the park system include Martin Luther King Jr. Memorial Park, Turning Point Park, and Tryon Park, which are located on former industrial and urban sites, in contrast to the early parks.[[164]](#cite_note-169) Most of the city's parks lie on the Genesee River and can be accessed by the Genesee Riverway Trail, which runs for the river's entire length through the city.[[165]](#cite_note-170) Current city facilities include 12 full-time recreation centers, 48 playgrounds, two artificial ice rinks, 74 softball/baseball fields, 42 tennis courts, four soccer fields, and 45 outdoor basketball courts.[[166]](#cite_note-171)

## Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Government")]

Further information: [List of mayors of Rochester, New York](/wiki/List_of_mayors_of_Rochester,_New_York "List of mayors of Rochester, New York")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/FederalBuildingRochesterNewYorkFrontView.JPG/250px-FederalBuildingRochesterNewYorkFrontView.JPG)](/wiki/File:FederalBuildingRochesterNewYorkFrontView.JPG)

[Rochester City Hall](/wiki/Rochester_City_Hall_(New_York) "Rochester City Hall (New York)")

Rochester is governed by a [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") serving as chief executive of city government and a city council consisting of four district members and five at-large members.[[167]](#cite_note-172) Rochester has had a [Strong mayor-council](/wiki/Strong_Mayor "Strong Mayor") form of government since the approval of its current charter in a referendum in 1984.[[168]](#cite_note-strongmayor-173) Administrative officers are appointed by the mayor and confirmed by the city council.[[169]](#cite_note-174) All city legislation is proposed and passed by the council, and subject to a [veto](/wiki/Veto "Veto") by the mayor, unless a majority of two-thirds approved it.[[170]](#cite_note-175) The current mayor is [Malik Evans](/wiki/Malik_Evans "Malik Evans"), serving since January 1, 2022.

Upon the city's charter in 1834, the mayor had few powers and most power was vested in the city council, which was composed of alderman representing individual wards.[[171]](#cite_note-176) The charter was frequently modified during the 19th century to expand or reduce the mayor's powers, until an 1898 act by the New York State Legislature centralized all appointments under the mayor. The city abolished the mayor's office and adopted a city manager system in a 1925 referendum, after which the mayor became a minor position and the city manager assumed executive authority, with both being chosen by the members of the city council.[[172]](#cite_note-177) A 1984 referendum restored the Strong mayor system.[[168]](#cite_note-strongmayor-173) [William A. Johnson](/wiki/William_A._Johnson_Jr. "William A. Johnson Jr."), the city's first African American mayor, served from 1994 to 2005. [Lovely Warren](/wiki/Lovely_Warren "Lovely Warren"), the city's first female mayor, served from 2014 to 2021.

### Courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Courts")]

Rochester is part of the 7th Judicial District of the [New York Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") and the 4th Department of the [New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division").

#### Rochester City Court

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Rochester City Court")]

Rochester City Court is part of the [New York State Unified Court System](/wiki/Judiciary_of_New_York "Judiciary of New York")[[179]](#cite_note-184) and consists of ten full-time judges, each of whom is elected to a 10-year term. Each judge must be a city resident and must have been an attorney in New York for at least five years.[[180]](#cite_note-185) Judges have a mandatory retirement age of 70. Vacancies on the court are filled by the mayor, and judges so appointed must run for a full term at the next general election.[[181]](#cite_note-186)

In New York State, the 61 [city courts](/wiki/City_courts "City courts")[[182]](#cite_note-187) outside of New York City handle the arraignment of felonies, try misdemeanors and lesser offenses, and try civil lawsuits involving claims of up to $15,000. Rochester City Court also hears small claims matters up to $5,000.[[183]](#cite_note-188) Rules of practice and procedure within all city courts are prescribed by the Uniform City Court Act.[[184]](#cite_note-189) Rochester City Court, like all city courts, follows the individual assignment system ("IAS"). This means that each case is assigned to a judge when the case is first initiated, and, with a few exceptions, stays under the supervision of that particular judge until the case is resolved.[[185]](#cite_note-190)

Created in 1876, the court was initially named the "Municipal Court of the City of Rochester" and had two judges.[[186]](#cite_note-191) Originally, city courts throughout the state were self-regulating, and prescribed their own rules of procedure and bounds of jurisdiction.[[187]](#cite_note-auto-192) Rochester City Court was governed by the Rochester City Court Act, which was a part of the Charter of the City of Rochester.[[188]](#cite_note-193) In 1935, Judge Jacob Gitelman introduced weekend sentencing. He was the first judge in New York State to do so.[[189]](#cite_note-194) In 1964, the New York State Constitution was amended to require uniform jurisdiction, practice, and procedure for the city courts, to be regulated by the state legislature.[[187]](#cite_note-auto-192) The court's first African-American judge, Reuben K. Davis, was appointed to the city court bench in March 1967.[[190]](#cite_note-195)

In the 1980s, the court heard cases involving the prosecution of the "Topfree Seven", women who intentionally bared their chests once a year in order to protest the criminalization of female nudity. Judge Herman J. Walz ruled that the women could not be prosecuted under New York's public nudity statute because their act of going topless in order to protest the law was imbued with First Amendment protections.[[191]](#cite_note-196) The decision was later affirmed by the [New York Court of Appeals](/wiki/New_York_Court_of_Appeals "New York Court of Appeals").[[192]](#cite_note-197)[[193]](#cite_note-198)[[194]](#cite_note-199) By 1995, Rochester City Court had eight judges. A ninth was added in 2001.[[195]](#cite_note-200) The court was brought to its current complement of ten judges in 2014.[[196]](#cite_note-201)

During the 2022–23 school year, 22,238 students were enrolled in the district, with an average expenditure per student of [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")23,266 and a four-year graduation rate of 71%.[[197]](#cite_note-202) The district is governed by a popularly elected seven-member board of education.[[198]](#cite_note-203) The Rochester City School District operates 14 public secondary schools, each serving grades 7 through 12.[[199]](#cite_note-204)

* Dr. Alice Holloway Young School of Excellence (7–8)
* [East Lower School](/wiki/East_High_School_(Rochester,_New_York) "East High School (Rochester, New York)") (6–8)

* Franklin Lower School (7–8)

* [James Monroe Lower School](/wiki/James_Monroe_High_School_(Rochester,_New_York) "James Monroe High School (Rochester, New York)") (7–8)
* [James Monroe Upper School](/wiki/James_Monroe_High_School_(Rochester,_New_York) "James Monroe High School (Rochester, New York)") (9–12)
* [Joseph C. Wilson Magnet High School](/wiki/Wilson_Magnet_High_School "Wilson Magnet High School") (9–12)

For the 2022–23 school year, Rochester additionally had fourteen free [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") serving grades Kindergarten through 12.[[200]](#cite_note-205) Other private schools in and near Rochester include [McQuaid Jesuit High School](/wiki/McQuaid_Jesuit_High_School "McQuaid Jesuit High School"), [Aquinas Institute](/wiki/Aquinas_Institute "Aquinas Institute") and [Bishop Kearny High School](/wiki/Bishop_Kearney_High_School_(Irondequoit,_New_York) "Bishop Kearney High School (Irondequoit, New York)").

### Colleges and universities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Colleges and universities")]

For a complete list of institutions of higher learning in the metropolitan area, see [Rochester, New York metropolitan area § Colleges and universities](/wiki/Rochester,_New_York_metropolitan_area#Colleges_and_universities "Rochester, New York metropolitan area").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/RushRhees.jpg/250px-RushRhees.jpg)](/wiki/File:RushRhees.jpg)

[Rush Rhees Library](/wiki/Rush_Rhees_Library "Rush Rhees Library") at the [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Colgate_Rochester_Crozer_Divinity_School1.jpg/250px-Colgate_Rochester_Crozer_Divinity_School1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colgate_Rochester_Crozer_Divinity_School1.jpg)

[Colgate Rochester Crozer Divinity School](/wiki/Colgate_Rochester_Crozer_Divinity_School "Colgate Rochester Crozer Divinity School")

Rochester and the surrounding region host a high concentration of colleges and universities, which drive much of the economic growth in the five-county area. The [University of Rochester](/wiki/University_of_Rochester "University of Rochester") is the only large research institution primarily within the city limits, although [Monroe Community College](/wiki/Monroe_Community_College "Monroe Community College") and [SUNY Brockport](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_at_Brockport "State University of New York at Brockport") operate campuses [downtown](/wiki/Downtown_Rochester "Downtown Rochester"). The Highland Park neighborhood was home to [Colgate Rochester Crozer Divinity School](/wiki/Colgate_Rochester_Crozer_Divinity_School "Colgate Rochester Crozer Divinity School") (part of whose facility is leased by [Ithaca College](/wiki/Ithaca_College "Ithaca College")'s Department of Physical Therapy) and an office maintained by the [Cornell University School of Industrial and Labor Relations](/wiki/Cornell_University_School_of_Industrial_and_Labor_Relations "Cornell University School of Industrial and Labor Relations").

The University of Rochester is the metropolitan area's oldest and most prominent institution of higher learning, and one of the country's top research centers. It includes a nursing school, the [Simon School of Business](/wiki/William_E._Simon_Graduate_School_of_Business_Administration "William E. Simon Graduate School of Business Administration"), and the [Eastman School of Music](/wiki/Eastman_School_of_Music "Eastman School of Music"). It was founded and endowed by [George Eastman](/wiki/George_Eastman "George Eastman") in his years as a [philanthropist](/wiki/Philanthropy "Philanthropy").[[201]](#cite_note-206) He also contributed greatly to the University of Rochester from wealth based on the success of Eastman Kodak.

Five institutions began operations in the city and later moved to Rochester's inner-ring suburbs: the [Empire State College](/wiki/Empire_State_College "Empire State College") Rochester Learning Center,[[202]](#cite_note-207) [Monroe Community College](/wiki/Monroe_Community_College "Monroe Community College"),[[203]](#cite_note-208) [Rochester Institute of Technology](/wiki/Rochester_Institute_of_Technology "Rochester Institute of Technology"),[[204]](#cite_note-209) [St. Bernard's School of Theology and Ministry](/wiki/St._Bernard%27s_School_of_Theology_and_Ministry "St. Bernard's School of Theology and Ministry") and [Nazareth University](/wiki/Nazareth_University "Nazareth University"). Rochester was the host of the [Barleywood Female University](/wiki/Barleywood_Female_University "Barleywood Female University"), a short-lived women's college from 1852 to 1853. The Lutheran seminary that became [Wagner College](/wiki/Wagner_College "Wagner College") was established in the city in 1883 and remained for some 35 years before moving to Staten Island.[[205]](#cite_note-210)

### Libraries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Libraries")]

The Rochester Public Library is headquartered at the Central Library of Rochester & Monroe County, which is located in the [Rundel Memorial Library](/wiki/Rundel_Memorial_Library "Rundel Memorial Library") building and the adjacent Bausch & Lomb Public Library Building in downtown Rochester. The city is part of the larger Monroe County Library System. Library cards are freely available to residents of Monroe County and valid at the eleven branch libraries in the city and other branches in the county.[[206]](#cite_note-211) The library had 8.1 visits per capita and over 6,000 programs as of 2018.[[207]](#cite_note-212)

## Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Media")]

Main article: [Media in Rochester, New York](/wiki/Media_in_Rochester,_New_York "Media in Rochester, New York")

The *[Democrat and Chronicle](/wiki/Democrat_and_Chronicle "Democrat and Chronicle"),* a [Gannett](/wiki/Gannett "Gannett") newspaper*,* is Rochester's main daily newspaper. Other local publications exist which cater to special interests, such as the *Rochester Business Journal*[[208]](#cite_note-213) and the *Minority Reporter*.[[209]](#cite_note-214) Former publications serving the city include *Insider* magazine, the *Rochester Post Express*,[[210]](#cite_note-215) the *Rochester Evening Journal*,[[211]](#cite_note-216) and the *[Times-Union](/wiki/Rochester_Times-Union "Rochester Times-Union")*.

Rochester is also served by several local television and radio stations, with [WROC-TV](/wiki/WROC-TV "WROC-TV") as the oldest television station serving the Rochester metro area.[[212]](#cite_note-217) The [WXXI Public Broadcasting Council](/wiki/WXXI_Public_Broadcasting_Council "WXXI Public Broadcasting Council") is a non-profit organization in Rochester which provides public television and community radio programs. WXXI owns or operates several radio stations, the television station [WXXI-TV](/wiki/WXXI-TV "WXXI-TV"), the alternative weekly publication *[City](/wiki/City_Newspaper "City Newspaper")* Magazine, and the [Little Theater](/wiki/Little_Theatre_(Rochester,_New_York) "Little Theatre (Rochester, New York)").[[213]](#cite_note-218)

Several movies have been filmed at least in part in Rochester, including *[The Amazing Spiderman 2](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spiderman_2 "The Amazing Spiderman 2")* (2014) and *[The Tomorrow Man](/wiki/The_Tomorrow_Man "The Tomorrow Man")* (2019).[[214]](#cite_note-imdb.com-219)
