#### Maritime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Maritime")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/RochesterCanal_RochesterNY.jpg/250px-RochesterCanal_RochesterNY.jpg)](/wiki/File:RochesterCanal_RochesterNY.jpg)

Packet boats on the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River")

There is marine freight service at the [Port of Rochester](/wiki/Charlotte,_Rochester,_New_York "Charlotte, Rochester, New York") on Lake Ontario, which is connected to the Atlantic Ocean via the [Saint Lawrence Seaway](/wiki/Saint_Lawrence_Seaway "Saint Lawrence Seaway"). The Erie Canal intersects the Genesee River on the south side of the city.

During the 19th and early 20th centuries, a number of passenger ferries operated on Lake Ontario between the Port of Rochester and Canada.[[215]](#cite_note-220) Service ended in 1950 when the *Ontario I* and *Ontario II* ended their route between Rochester and [Cobourg](/wiki/Cobourg "Cobourg").[[216]](#cite_note-221) A new ferry, the [Spirit of Ontario I](/wiki/HSC_Virgen_de_Coromoto "HSC Virgen de Coromoto"), operated between Rochester and Toronto from June 17, 2004, to December 12, 2005. The ferry suffered from numerous issues, including two separate pier collisions that damaged it. The initial operator, [Canadian American Transportation Systems](/wiki/Canadian_American_Transportation_Systems "Canadian American Transportation Systems") (CATS), went bankrupt by the end of the 2004 season. The city of Rochester then purchased the ferry and signed a contract with [Bay Ferries Great Lakes](/wiki/Bay_Ferries_Great_Lakes "Bay Ferries Great Lakes") to resume operations in 2005. The resumption of service was delayed until summer, causing the ferry to continue to operate at a loss. In 2006, the operation was shut down and the ferry was sold.[[217]](#cite_note-222)

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Air")]

Main article: [Greater Rochester International Airport](/wiki/Greater_Rochester_International_Airport "Greater Rochester International Airport")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Greater_Rochester_International_Airport_May_2007_Aerial_View.jpg/250px-Greater_Rochester_International_Airport_May_2007_Aerial_View.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greater_Rochester_International_Airport_May_2007_Aerial_View.jpg)

Aerial view of the [Greater Rochester International Airport](/wiki/Greater_Rochester_International_Airport "Greater Rochester International Airport")

Rochester is served by the Frederick Douglass Greater Rochester International Airport (GRIA). Scheduled air service is provided by [American](/wiki/American_Airlines "American Airlines"), [Allegiant](/wiki/Allegiant_Airlines "Allegiant Airlines"), [Delta](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines"), [Frontier](/wiki/Frontier_Airlines "Frontier Airlines"), [JetBlue](/wiki/JetBlue_Airways "JetBlue Airways"), [Southwest](/wiki/Southwest_Airlines "Southwest Airlines"), [Spirit](/wiki/Spirit_Airlines "Spirit Airlines"), and [United](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines").[[218]](#cite_note-223)

[FedEx](/wiki/FedEx "FedEx") founder [Fred Smith](/wiki/Frederick_W._Smith "Frederick W. Smith") has stated in several articles that [Xerox](/wiki/Xerox "Xerox")'s development of the copier, and its need to quickly get parts to customers, was one of the economic issues that led him to pioneer the overnight delivery business in 1971.[[219]](#cite_note-224)[[220]](#cite_note-225) Because Xerox manufactured its copiers in Rochester,[[221]](#cite_note-226) the city was one of the original 25 cities FedEx served on its first night of operations on April 17, 1973.[[222]](#cite_note-227)

In 2016, Governor [Andrew Cuomo](/wiki/Andrew_Cuomo "Andrew Cuomo") announced a $63.4 million project to renovate the GRIA.[[223]](#cite_note-228) The renovations include a large canopy extending over both main entrances, solar panels, a rainwater collection system, and modern communication and security enhancements.[[224]](#cite_note-229) All construction was completed by October 2018.[[225]](#cite_note-230)

#### Rails and mass transit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Rails and mass transit")]

See also: [Louise M. Slaughter Rochester Station](/wiki/Louise_M._Slaughter_Rochester_Station "Louise M. Slaughter Rochester Station") and [Rochester Subway](/wiki/Rochester_Subway "Rochester Subway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Rochester_platform_2019.jpg/250px-Rochester_platform_2019.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochester_platform_2019.jpg)

Platform at the [Louise M. Slaughter Rochester Station](/wiki/Louise_M._Slaughter_Rochester_Station "Louise M. Slaughter Rochester Station")

Local bus service in Rochester and its county suburbs is provided by the [Rochester-Genesee Regional Transportation Authority](/wiki/Rochester-Genesee_Regional_Transportation_Authority "Rochester-Genesee Regional Transportation Authority") (RGRTA) via its Regional Transit Service (RTS) subsidiary. RTS also provides suburban service outside the immediate Rochester area and runs smaller transportation systems in outlying counties, such as WATS (Wayne Area Transportation System). All RTS routes are based out of the RTS Transit Center on Mortimer Street. Rochester has intercity and transcontinental bus service via [Greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound_Lines "Greyhound Lines") and [Trailways](/wiki/Trailways_Transportation_System "Trailways Transportation System").[[226]](#cite_note-231)

Rail service to Rochester is provided by the Louise M. Slaughter Rochester Station, served by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak")'s *[Empire Service](/wiki/Empire_Service "Empire Service")* between New York City and Niagara Falls, the *[Maple Leaf](/wiki/Maple_Leaf_(train) "Maple Leaf (train)")* between New York City and Toronto, and the *[Lake Shore Limited](/wiki/Lake_Shore_Limited "Lake Shore Limited")* between New York City/Boston and Chicago. Prior to 1965, Rochester had a smaller station reminiscent of New York City's "[Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal")". It was among [Claude Fayette Bragdon](/wiki/Claude_Fayette_Bragdon "Claude Fayette Bragdon")'s best works in Rochester.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The current station is modeled after Bragdon's work and named in honor of former longtime congresswoman [Louise Slaughter](/wiki/Louise_Slaughter "Louise Slaughter").[[227]](#cite_note-232)

Rochester used to be a major stop on several railroad lines. The [New York Central Railroad](/wiki/New_York_Central_Railroad "New York Central Railroad") provided service to Chicago and Buffalo to the west and Albany and New York City to the east and southeast. The [Buffalo, Rochester and Pittsburgh Railway](/wiki/Buffalo,_Rochester_and_Pittsburgh_Railway "Buffalo, Rochester and Pittsburgh Railway") (absorbed by the [Baltimore and Ohio Railroad](/wiki/Baltimore_and_Ohio_Railroad "Baltimore and Ohio Railroad")) served Buffalo and Pittsburgh until 1955. A rail route to Salamanca in southern New York State afforded connections in [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca,_New_York "Salamanca, New York") to southwestern and southeastern New York State.[[228]](#cite_note-233)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] The last long-distance train was the *Northern Express*/*Southern Express,* operated by the [Pennsylvania Railroad](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Railroad "Pennsylvania Railroad") on the [Genesee Valley Canal Railroad](/wiki/Genesee_Valley_Canal_Railroad "Genesee Valley Canal Railroad"), that went to [Harrisburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania") via Canandaigua, Elmira and Williamsport; service ended in 1971.[[229]](#cite_note-234) Also serving Rochester were the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") and [Lehigh Valley Railroad](/wiki/Lehigh_Valley_Railroad "Lehigh Valley Railroad").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Rochester_NY_Broad_Street_Bridge_Subway_2001.jpg/250px-Rochester_NY_Broad_Street_Bridge_Subway_2001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rochester_NY_Broad_Street_Bridge_Subway_2001.jpg)

The Broad Street Aqueduct was used as a [subway tunnel](/wiki/Rochester_Subway "Rochester Subway") in the mid-20th century.

From 1927 to 1956, Rochester had a [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") underground transit system called the [Rochester subway](/wiki/Rochester_subway "Rochester subway"), which was first operated by [New York State Railways](/wiki/New_York_State_Railways "New York State Railways") and later by the [Rochester Transit Corporation](/wiki/Rochester_Transit_Corporation "Rochester Transit Corporation").[[230]](#cite_note-235)[[40]](#cite_note-subway-41) Rochester was the smallest city in the world to have such a system.[[231]](#cite_note-236) After the subway was shut down in 1956, the eastern half of the subway past Court Street became the [Eastern Expressway](/wiki/Eastern_Expressway "Eastern Expressway"), and the western end of the open cut was filled in 1976. The tunnel was last used for freight service by [Gannett Company](/wiki/Gannett_Company "Gannett Company") to bring paper to the printing presses for the [Democrat and Chronicle](/wiki/Democrat_and_Chronicle "Democrat and Chronicle") in 1997.[[232]](#cite_note-237) In the years since, the tunnel has become a hub for [graffiti](/wiki/Graffiti "Graffiti") artists. Several proposals have been made to completely fill the remaining tunnel, redevelop the underground space, or convert the bridge to a pedestrian crossing. Portions have been filled in at the western end and the eastern end for new above-ground development.[[233]](#cite_note-238)[[234]](#cite_note-239) The [Broad Street aqueduct](/wiki/Broad_Street_Bridge_(Rochester,_New_York) "Broad Street Bridge (Rochester, New York)"), which contains part of the tunnel, was added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1976.[[235]](#cite_note-240)

#### Major highways and roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Major highways and roads")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/RocNight_Rochester_at_Night%2C_October_1st_2016_%2830057483855%29.jpg/250px-RocNight_Rochester_at_Night%2C_October_1st_2016_%2830057483855%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:RocNight_Rochester_at_Night,_October_1st_2016_(30057483855).jpg)

[Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass%E2%80%93Susan_B._Anthony_Memorial_Bridge "Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge") on [Interstate 490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)")

Three exits off the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway") ([I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York")) serve Rochester. Rochester's expressway system, conceived in the 1950s, was designed as two concentric circles with feeder expressways from the west, south and east. The system allows for quick travel within the metropolitan area and a lack of the traffic gridlock typically found in cities of comparable size; in part this is because the system was designed to accommodate rapid travel between the suburbs and downtown,[[236]](#cite_note-241) and also because it was built when the city's population was over 330,000, whereas today it is a full third less.[[237]](#cite_note-242)

The [Outer Loop](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_47 "New York State Route 47") circles just outside the city limits while the former [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)") once circled around the immediate downtown area within the city (the easternmost sector was closed in 2015). From the west are [Lake Ontario State Parkway](/wiki/Lake_Ontario_State_Parkway "Lake Ontario State Parkway"), [NY 531](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_531 "New York State Route 531") and [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)"); [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") feeds from the south; and [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104"), [NY 441](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_441 "New York State Route 441"), and [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)") approach from the east.

In the early 1970s, the Genesee Expressway Task Force, City leaders, and the New York State Department of Transportation studied the feasibility of connecting the outer and inner Loops with a new southern expressway. The proposed route extended north from the I-390 and I-590 interchange in Brighton, cutting through Rochester's Swillburg neighborhood. In 1972, consultants Berger Lehman Associates recommended a new 'Busway', an expressway with dedicated bus lanes, similar to Bus Rapid Transit.[[238]](#cite_note-243) The expressway extension was never built. In 2016, the City of Rochester launched the [Pace Car Program](/wiki/Pace_Car_Program "Pace Car Program").[[239]](#cite_note-244)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/I-390.svg/40px-I-390.svg.png)](/wiki/File:I-390.svg) [I-390](/wiki/Interstate_390 "Interstate 390") (Genesee Expressway)

* I-390 runs south–north, crossing [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York") (exit 46) and routing north through Rochester's western suburbs. Its northern end is at [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)"), however, it continues north as [NY 390](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_390 "New York State Route 390") until it merges into the [Lake Ontario State Parkway](/wiki/Lake_Ontario_State_Parkway "Lake Ontario State Parkway"). South of I-90, I-390 runs to [Avoca](/wiki/Avoca,_New_York "Avoca, New York"), where it meets with [US 15](/wiki/U.S._Route_15_in_New_York "U.S. Route 15 in New York") and the Southern Tier Expressway, [I-86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania-New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania-New York)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/I-490.svg/40px-I-490.svg.png)](/wiki/File:I-490.svg) [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)") (Western/Eastern Expressway)

* I-490 runs west–east through Rochester, starting at [Le Roy](/wiki/Le_Roy,_New_York "Le Roy, New York") and ending in [Victor](/wiki/Victor,_New_York "Victor, New York"). It interchanges with the two other Interstates in Rochester: I-390 at the western city limit and [I-590](/wiki/Interstate_590 "Interstate 590") at the eastern limit, as well as connecting at both ends with the Thruway, [I-90](/wiki/Interstate_90_in_New_York "Interstate 90 in New York") (exits 47 and 45). In July 2007, a new bridge over the [Genesee River](/wiki/Genesee_River "Genesee River") was completed and named the [Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass%E2%80%93Susan_B._Anthony_Memorial_Bridge "Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/I-590.svg/40px-I-590.svg.png)](/wiki/File:I-590.svg) [I-590](/wiki/Interstate_590 "Interstate 590")

* I-590 runs south–north through Rochester's eastern suburbs. Its southern end is at I-390, while the northern terminus is at [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)"); the highway continues north to the shore of Lake Ontario as [NY 590](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_590 "New York State Route 590").
* In decreasing usage is the term "[Can of Worms](/wiki/Can_of_Worms_(interchange) "Can of Worms (interchange)")", referring to the previously dangerous at-grade intersection of [I-490](/wiki/Interstate_490_(New_York) "Interstate 490 (New York)") and expressway [NY 590](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_590 "New York State Route 590") on the eastern edge of the Rochester city limits, bordering the suburb of [Brighton](/wiki/Brighton,_Monroe_County,_New_York "Brighton, Monroe County, New York"). In the 1980s, a multimillion-dollar project created a system of overpasses and ramps that reduced the danger but resulted in the loss of certain exits.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/NY-104.svg/40px-NY-104.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NY-104.svg) [NY 104](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_104 "New York State Route 104") (Irondequoit-Wayne County Expressway, West Ridge Road)

* NY 104 – Just east of the NY 590 interchange, NY 104 becomes the Irondequoit-Wayne County Expressway and crosses the [Irondequoit Bay Bridge](/wiki/Irondequoit_Bay_Bridge "Irondequoit Bay Bridge"). On the other side of the Bay Bridge, in the town of Webster, NY 104 has exits before returning to an at-grade highway at Basket Road.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/NY-390.svg/40px-NY-390.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NY-390.svg) [NY 390](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_390 "New York State Route 390")

* NY 390 is an extension of Interstate 390 from the I-390/I-490 interchange in Gates. The northern terminus is at the Lake Ontario State Parkway in Greece, less than a mile from the Lake Ontario shoreline.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/NY-590.svg/40px-NY-590.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NY-590.svg) [NY 590](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_590 "New York State Route 590")

* NY 590 is a limited-access extension of Interstate 590 that runs from an interchange between Interstate 490 and I-590 on the Brighton/Rochester border. The northern terminus is at Culver Road in Irondequoit, near Sea Breeze (the western shore of Irondequoit Bay at Lake Ontario).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Rochester_Inner_Loop.svg/40px-Rochester_Inner_Loop.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rochester_Inner_Loop.svg) [Inner Loop](/wiki/Inner_Loop_(Rochester) "Inner Loop (Rochester)")

* The Inner Loop Runs from I-490 to Main Street on the north end and from 490 to Monroe Avenue at the south end. Formerly a loop, the eastern end was demolished and replaced with a surface road between 2014 and 2017. Unsigned reference NY 940T begins and ends at I-490, and the rest of the Loop is part of I-490 between exits 13 and 15, including the [Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge](/wiki/Frederick_Douglass%E2%80%93Susan_B._Anthony_Memorial_Bridge "Frederick Douglass–Susan B. Anthony Memorial Bridge"). This expressway is commonly used to define the borders of Downtown Rochester.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/Lake_Ontario_State_Pkwy_Shield.svg/40px-Lake_Ontario_State_Pkwy_Shield.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lake_Ontario_State_Pkwy_Shield.svg) [Lake Ontario State Parkway](/wiki/Lake_Ontario_State_Parkway "Lake Ontario State Parkway")

* Lake Ontario State Parkway travels from [Lakeside Beach State Park](/wiki/Lakeside_Beach_State_Park "Lakeside Beach State Park") in Carlton, Orleans County. The eastern end is at Lake Avenue in the city of Rochester in Monroe County.

### Public safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Public safety")]

Fire protection and [EMS](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services") services are provided by the Rochester Fire Department. The department employs 509 personnel[[240]](#cite_note-firebudget-245) and operates thirteen engines, six trucks, and heavy rescue from fifteen [fire stations](/wiki/Fire_station "Fire station").[[241]](#cite_note-246) In FY 2021–22, the department responded to 38,876 incidents.[[240]](#cite_note-firebudget-245) The current fire chief is Stefano Napolitano.[[242]](#cite_note-247)

Law enforcement services are provided by the [Rochester Police Department](/wiki/Rochester_Police_Department "Rochester Police Department"). The current chief of police is David Smith.[[243]](#cite_note-248) Independent oversight of the department is provided by the Police Accountability Board, established in 2019 to investigate and discipline officers for misconduct. In 2023, the New York Court of Appeals ruled that the board could not enforce disciplinary actions on any officers in the department, as it would violate the contract between the city and police union.[[244]](#cite_note-249)

In 2019, Rochester had 1,540 reported violent crimes. That same year, Rochester had 7,142 property crime incidents. These included 33 [murders](/wiki/Murder "Murder"), 429 robberies, 976 [aggravated assaults](/wiki/Aggravated_assault "Aggravated assault"), 1,269 [burglaries](/wiki/Burglary "Burglary"), 5,222 [larceny](/wiki/Larceny "Larceny") thefts, 102 forcible [rapes](/wiki/Rape "Rape"), 651 [auto thefts](/wiki/Auto_theft "Auto theft"), and 83 acts of [arson](/wiki/Arson "Arson").[[245]](#cite_note-crime-250)

On November 12, 2021, Rochester Mayor [Lovely Warren](/wiki/Lovely_Warren "Lovely Warren") declared a [state of emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency") due to a rising violent crime rate in the city, which produced 81 homicides by December 31, the highest number of any year on record. Additional law enforcement assistance was requested from, and granted by, the state government.[[246]](#cite_note-251) On July 21, 2022, Rochester Mayor [Malik Evans](/wiki/Malik_Evans "Malik Evans") declared another state of emergency due to ongoing gun violence.[[247]](#cite_note-252)

Rochester experienced a decline in its violent crime rate in both 2023 and 2024. In 2024, there were 37 reported homicide incidents (there were 45 victims), a 26 percent drop compared with 2023. There was also a sharp decline in automotive vehicle thefts in 2024—2,068 reported incidents, down from the record 3,943 in 2023. Overall, property crime incidents were down 33 percent in 2024 compared to 2023.[[248]](#cite_note-253)

### Healthcare

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Rochester,_New_York&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Healthcare")]

Most healthcare services in the Rochester area are provided by [University of Rochester Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Rochester_Medical_Center "University of Rochester Medical Center") (URMC) and [Rochester Regional Health](/wiki/Rochester_Regional_Health "Rochester Regional Health") (RRH). Major hospitals in the Rochester area include:

* [Highland Hospital](/wiki/Highland_Hospital_(Rochester,_New_York) "Highland Hospital (Rochester, New York)"), a URMC facility with 261 beds. Highland is a level 1 perinatal center and a primary stroke center.[[249]](#cite_note-254)
* [Rochester General Hospital](/wiki/Rochester_General_Hospital "Rochester General Hospital"), a RRH facility with 528 beds. Rochester General is a comprehensive stroke center and level 2 perinatal center.[[250]](#cite_note-255)
* [Rochester Psychiatric Center](/wiki/Rochester_Psychiatric_Center "Rochester Psychiatric Center"), a psychiatric hospital operated by the [New York State Department of Mental Hygiene](/wiki/New_York_State_Office_of_Mental_Health "New York State Office of Mental Health").[[251]](#cite_note-256) The hospital's campus contains the abandoned [Terrence Building](/wiki/Terrence_Building "Terrence Building").
* [Strong Memorial Hospital](/wiki/Strong_Memorial_Hospital "Strong Memorial Hospital"), a URMC facility with 886 beds. Strong is home to the [Golisano Children's Hospital](/wiki/Golisano_Children%27s_Hospital_(Rochester,_NY) "Golisano Children's Hospital (Rochester, NY)") for pediatric care, and is an AIDS center, burn center, comprehensive stroke center, level I trauma center for both pediatric and adult care, and regional perinatal center.[[252]](#cite_note-257)
* [Unity Hospital](/wiki/Unity_Hospital "Unity Hospital"), a RRH facility with 311 beds. Unity is a regional perinatal center and primary stroke center.[[253]](#cite_note-258)

In 2023, *[The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian")* reported that Rochester was becoming a desirable community for [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") individuals due to its network of gender-affirming care providers, such as Trillium Health.[[254]](#cite_note-259)
